title: XII: A Morning Visitor
html:
XII

A Morning Visitor

All through the night, waking and sleeping, and in my dreams, I wondered what Marjorie could see in him! In those same dreams I satisfied myself that she could, and did, see nothing in him, but everything in meÔªø‚Äîoh the comfort! The misfortune was that when I awoke I knew it was the other way roundÔªø‚Äîso that it was a sad awakening. An awakening to thoughts of murder.

So, swallowing a mouthful and a peg, I went into my laboratory to plan murderÔªø‚Äîlegalised murderÔªø‚Äîon the biggest scale it ever has been planned. I was on the track of a weapon which would make war not only an affair of a single campaign, but of a single half-hour. It would not want an army to work it either. Once let an individual, or two or three at most, in possession of my weapon-that-was-to-be, get within a mile or so of even the largest body of disciplined troops that ever yet a nation put into the field, andÔªø‚Äîpouf!Ôªø‚Äîin about the time it takes you to say that they would be all dead men. If weapons of precision, which may be relied upon to slay, are preservers of the peaceÔªø‚Äîand the man is a fool who says that they are not!Ôªø‚Äîthen I was within reach of the finest preserver of the peace imagination ever yet conceived.

What a sublime thought to think that in the hollow of your own hand lies the life and death of nationsÔªø‚Äîand it was almost in mine.

I had in front of me some of the finest destructive agents you could wish to light uponÔªø‚Äîcarbon-monoxide, chlorine-trioxide, mercuric-oxide, conine, potassamide, potassium-carboxide, cyanogenÔªø‚Äîwhen Edwards entered. I was wearing a mask of my own invention, a thing that covered ears and head and everything, something like a diver‚Äôs helmetÔªø‚ÄîI was dealing with gases a sniff of which meant death; only a few days before, unmasked, I had been doing some fool‚Äôs trick with a couple of acidsÔªø‚Äîsulphuric and cyanide of potassiumÔªø‚Äîwhen, somehow, my hand slipped, and, before I knew it, minute portions of them combined. By the mercy of Providence I fell backwards instead of forwards;Ôªø‚Äîsequel, about an hour afterwards Edwards found me on the floor, and it took the remainder of that day, and most of the doctors in town, to bring me back to life again.

Edwards announced his presence by touching me on the shoulderÔªø‚Äîwhen I am wearing that mask it isn‚Äôt always easy to make me hear.

‚ÄúSomeone wishes to see you, sir.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen tell someone that I don‚Äôt wish to see him.‚Äù

Well-trained servant, EdwardsÔªø‚Äîhe walked off with the message as decorously as you please. And then I thought there was an endÔªø‚Äîbut there wasn‚Äôt.

I was regulating the valve of a cylinder in which I was fusing some oxides when, once more, someone touched me on the shoulder. Without turning I took it for granted it was Edwards back again.

‚ÄúI have only to give a tiny twist to this tap, my good fellow, and you will be in the land where the bogies bloom. Why will you come where you‚Äôre not wanted?‚Äù Then I looked round. ‚ÄúWho the devil are you?‚Äù

For it was not Edwards at all, but quite a different class of character.

I found myself confronting an individual who might almost have sat for one of the bogies I had just alluded to. His costume was reminiscent of the ‚ÄúAlgerians‚Äù whom one finds all over France, and who are the most persistent, insolent and amusing of pedlars. I remember one who used to haunt the r√©p√©titions at the Alcazar at ToursÔªø‚Äîbut there! This individual was like the originals, yet unlikeÔªø‚Äîhe was less gaudy, and a good deal dingier, than his Gallic prototypes are apt to be. Then he wore a burnooseÔªø‚Äîthe yellow, grimy-looking article of the Arab of the Sudan, not the spick and span Arab of the boulevard. Chief difference of all, his face was clean shavenÔªø‚Äîand whoever saw an Algerian of Paris whose chiefest glory was not his well-trimmed moustache and beard?

I expected that he would address me in the lingo which these gentlemen call FrenchÔªø‚Äîbut he didn‚Äôt.

‚ÄúYou are Mr.¬ÝAtherton?‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd you are Mr.Ôªø‚ÄîWho?Ôªø‚Äîhow did you come here? Where‚Äôs my servant?‚Äù

The fellow held up his hand. As he did so, as if in accordance with a prearranged signal, Edwards came into the room looking excessively startled. I turned to him.

‚ÄúIs this the person who wished to see me?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, sir.‚Äù

‚ÄúDidn‚Äôt I tell you to say that I didn‚Äôt wish to see him?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, sir.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen why didn‚Äôt you do as I told you?‚Äù

‚ÄúI did, sir.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen how comes he here?‚Äù

‚ÄúReally, sir,‚ÄùÔªø‚ÄîEdwards put his hand up to his head as if he was half asleepÔªø‚Äî‚ÄúI don‚Äôt quite know.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat do you mean by you don‚Äôt know? Why didn‚Äôt you stop him?‚Äù

‚ÄúI think, sir, that I must have had a touch of sudden faintness, because I tried to put out my hand to stop him, andÔªø‚ÄîI couldn‚Äôt.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou‚Äôre an idiot.Ôªø‚ÄîGo!‚Äù And he went. I turned to the stranger. ‚ÄúPray, sir, are you a magician?‚Äù

He replied to my question with another.

‚ÄúYou, Mr.¬ÝAthertonÔªø‚Äîare you also a magician?‚Äù

He was staring at my mask with an evident lack of comprehension.

‚ÄúI wear this because, in this place, death lurks in so many subtle forms, that, without it, I dare not breathe,‚Äù He inclined his headÔªø‚Äîthough I doubt if he understood. ‚ÄúBe so good as to tell me, briefly, what it is you wish with me.‚Äù

He slipped his hand into the folds of his burnoose, and, taking out a slip of paper, laid it on the shelf by which we were standing. I glanced at it, expecting to find on it a petition, or a testimonial, or a true statement of his sad case; instead it contained two words onlyÔªø‚Äî‚ÄúMarjorie Lindon.‚Äù The unlooked-for sight of that well-loved name brought the blood into my cheeks.

‚ÄúYou come from Miss Lindon?‚Äù He narrowed his shoulders, brought his fingertips together, inclined his head, in a fashion which was peculiarly Oriental, but not particularly explanatoryÔªø‚Äîso I repeated my question.

‚ÄúDo you wish me to understand that you do come from Miss Lindon?‚Äù

Again he slipped his hand into his burnoose, again he produced a slip of paper, again he laid it on the shelf, again I glanced at it, again nothing was written on it but a nameÔªø‚Äî‚ÄúPaul Lessingham.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell?Ôªø‚ÄîI seeÔªø‚ÄîPaul Lessingham.Ôªø‚ÄîWhat then?‚Äù

‚ÄúShe is goodÔªø‚Äîhe is badÔªø‚Äîis it not so?‚Äù

He touched first one scrap of paper, then the other. I stared.

‚ÄúPray how do you happen to know?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe shall never have herÔªø‚Äîeh?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat on earth do you mean?‚Äù

‚ÄúAh!Ôªø‚Äîwhat do I mean!‚Äù

‚ÄúPrecisely, what do you mean? And also, and at the same time, who the devil are you?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is as a friend I come to you.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen in that case you may go; I happen to be overstocked in that line just now.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot with the kind of friend I am!‚Äù

‚ÄúThe saints forefend!‚Äù

‚ÄúYou love herÔªø‚Äîyou love Miss Lindon! Can you bear to think of him in her arms?‚Äù

I took off my maskÔªø‚Äîfeeling that the occasion required it. As I did so he brushed aside the hanging folds of the hood of his burnoose, so that I saw more of his face. I was immediately conscious that in his eyes there was, in an especial degree, what, for want of a better term, one may call the mesmeric quality. That his was one of those morbid organisations which are oftener found, thank goodness, in the east than in the west, and which are apt to exercise an uncanny influence over the weak and the foolish folk with whom they come in contactÔªø‚Äîthe kind of creature for whom it is always just as well to keep a seasoned rope close handy. I was, also, conscious that he was taking advantage of the removal of my mask to try his strength on meÔªø‚Äîthan which he could not have found a tougher job. The sensitive something which is found in the hypnotic subject happens, in me, to be wholly absent.

‚ÄúI see you are a mesmerist.‚Äù

He started.

‚ÄúI am nothingÔªø‚Äîa shadow!‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd I‚Äôm a scientist. I should like, with your permissionÔªø‚Äîor without it!Ôªø‚Äîto try an experiment or two on you.‚Äù

He moved further back. There came a gleam into his eyes which suggested that he possessed his hideous power to an unusual degreeÔªø‚Äîthat, in the estimation of his own people, he was qualified to take his standing as a regular devil-doctor.

‚ÄúWe will try experiments together, you and IÔªø‚Äîon Paul Lessingham.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy on him?‚Äù

‚ÄúYou do not know?‚Äù

‚ÄúI do not.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy do you lie to me?‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt lie to youÔªø‚ÄîI haven‚Äôt the faintest notion what is the nature of your interest in Mr.¬ÝLessingham.‚Äù

‚ÄúMy interest?Ôªø‚Äîthat is another thing; it is your interest of which we are speaking.‚Äù

‚ÄúPardon meÔªø‚Äîit is yours.‚Äù

‚ÄúListen! you love herÔªø‚Äîand he! But at a word from you he shall not have herÔªø‚Äînever! It is I who say itÔªø‚ÄîI!‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd, once more, sir, who are you?‚Äù

‚ÄúI am of the children of Isis!‚Äù

‚ÄúIs that so?Ôªø‚ÄîIt occurs to me that you have made a slight mistakeÔªø‚Äîthis is London, not a dog-hole in the desert.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo I not know?Ôªø‚Äîwhat does it matter?Ôªø‚Äîyou shall see! There will come a time when you will want meÔªø‚Äîyou will find that you cannot bear to think of him in her armsÔªø‚Äîher whom you love! You will call to me, and I shall come, and of Paul Lessingham there shall be an end.‚Äù

While I was wondering whether he was really as mad as he sounded, or whether he was some impudent charlatan who had an axe of his own to grind, and thought that he had found in me a grindstone, he had vanished from the room. I moved after him.

‚ÄúHang it all!Ôªø‚Äîstop!‚Äù I cried.

He must have made pretty good travelling, because, before I had a foot in the hall, I heard the front door slam, and, when I reached the street, intent on calling him back, neither to the right nor to the left was there a sign of him to be seen.
